elements.sent-2411-001: This study aims to investigate the feasibility of utilizing sophisticated image processing techniques for automated voter counting as a solution to problems related to manual vote counting during electoral processes.
elements.sent-2411-002: The goal of the study is to clarify how automated systems that make use of state-of-the-art technologies like OpenCV, CVZone and the MOG2 algorithm could significantly improve election operations' efficiency and transparency.
elements.sent-2411-003: The empirical results highlight the potential of automated voter counting to improve voting procedures and restore public faith in election results, especially in areas where trust is low.
elements.sent-2411-004: The study also stresses the use of strict measures, like the F1 score, to evaluate automated systems' accuracy in comparison to manual counting techniques in a methodical manner.
elements.sent-2411-005: This methodology enables a detailed comprehension of the differences in performance between automated and human counting techniques by providing a nuanced assessment.
elements.sent-2411-006: The incorporation of said measures serves to reinforce an extensive assessment structure, guaranteeing the legitimacy and dependability of automated voting systems inside the electoral sphere.
elements.sent-2411-007: Elections are essential for the functioning of democratic societies.
elements.sent-2411-008: Ensuring their fairness and transparency is crucial for maintaining the trust of the electorate .
elements.sent-2411-009: However, in many parts of the world, manual vote counting has proven to be a flawed system.
elements.sent-2411-010: The time-intensive nature of manual counting introduces human error, inefficiencies, and opportunities for manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-011: These issues often culminate in a loss of public confidence, particularly in under developing nations where election outcomes are frequently contested.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this study, It is investigated how modern technologies, particularly machine learning and image processing, can be applied to solve these issues.
elements.sent-2411-013: By automating the voter counting process, aimed to minimize human error and improve efficiency, accuracy, and transparency in electoral systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: This paper specifically focuses on Bangladesh as a case study, where electoral mistrust has become a prominent issue throughout the elections \cite{31 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The democratic process, as the foundation of modern societies, gives citizens the fundamental right to vote and actively participate in the choosing of their leaders.
elements.sent-2411-016: At the core of this democratic exercise lies the crucial need for accurate and efficient voter counting \cite{5 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Democracy depends on the integrity of elections, yet traditional manual vote counting is labour-intensive, and time-consuming, and it sometimes causes major delays in the reporting of results.
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, the potential for human error introduces a significant risk to the accuracy of the count, raising concerns about the transparency and fairness of the electoral process \cite{6 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Election results in Bangladesh and other nations have become less credible and accurate due to human vote counting.
elements.sent-2411-020: The discrepancy between election commission reports and public opinion has led to a serious crisis of trust in democracy in Bangladesh, as it has in many other places \cite{7 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Autonomous vote counting is essential for transparency and confidence since mistrust undermines democracy, particularly in Bangladesh.
elements.sent-2411-022: Automated voter counting systems, especially those incorporating image processing techniques and transparency measures like CCTV installations, hold the potential to revolutionize the electoral process\cite{8 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Technological innovations can speed up vote counting, reduce mistakes, increase transparency, simplify elections, and contribute to rebuilding public confidence in the political process.
elements.sent-2411-024: One example of this is IoT systems that combine CCTV with person recognition.
elements.sent-2411-025: A potential remedy for the problems associated with manual vote counting is automated voter counting that makes use of IoT and image processing \cite{9}\cite{10 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The use of image processing in this new method aims to capture ballot images, which could lower the time and expense of manual vote counting while enhancing accuracy, as proven in several studies.
elements.sent-2411-027: Lin et al. \cite{2 demonstrates how residual and convolutional layers are used by deep learning frameworks for video-based car counting.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Another study introduces an automatic measuring and counting procedure using multiple picture analyses, streamlining the process with minimal calibrations \cite{3 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In a different context, researchers propose a new method for detecting and counting trees in aerial images using image processing techniques and machine learning algorithms \cite{4 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Additionally, Arputhamoni and Saravanan \cite{1 introduces an intelligent online voting system uses image processing, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and biometrics, such as fingerprint and face recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This study attempts to decrease voter turnout by tackling electoral fraud in India and demonstrating how voter digitization can improve Bangladeshi election accuracy by using machine learning to enable transparent and reliable automated vote counting.
elements.sent-2411-032: A chronic mismatch between official voter turnout and public perception poses serious difficulties to Bangladesh's democracy and creates a crisis of faith in the political process.
elements.sent-2411-033: By adding to the time and chance of human error, manual vote counting exacerbates these problems.
elements.sent-2411-034: In order to give a dependable and transparent solution, this study attempts to investigate automated voter counting using cutting-edge image processing technology.
elements.sent-2411-035: This will finally restore legitimacy and trust in the electoral system and inspire Bangladeshi voters to have newfound hope in democracy.
elements.sent-2411-036: The study discusses the problems that the voting system in Bangladesh is facing, where a large discrepancy between recorded voter turnout and public opinion has resulted in a crisis of faith in free and fair elections.
elements.sent-2411-037: This confidence has been further undermined by the laborious and error-prone manual vote counting process.
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus, there is a pressing need for creative ways to improve the voting process's dependability and transparency.
elements.sent-2411-039: The goal of this research is to demonstrate the efficacy of an automated voter counting system that employs image processing techniques in lowering errors and regaining public confidence.
elements.sent-2411-040: This thesis advances the conversation on electoral reform by highlighting the function of technology in fostering integrity and transparency.
elements.sent-2411-041: The primary research questions were:\par RQ1:Can an automated voter counting system using machine learning and image processing serve as a reliable alternative to manual vote counting?\par RQ2:How does the adoption of such a system improve public trust in the electoral process, especially in countries like Bangladesh with significant trust deficits?\par.
elements.sent-2411-042: The primary objectives of this research are:.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: To develop and test an automated voter counting system using advanced image processing technologies such as OpenCV, CVZone, and the MOG2 algorithm.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: To compare the system’s performance in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and speed against traditional manual vote counting.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: To evaluate the broader impact of the system on public trust and electoral transparency.
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: To explore the scalability of the system for use in national elections, especially in regions with significant political and social challenges.

elements.sent-2411-059: OpenCV is used as an open source platform along with python library CVZone, and algorithms are Haar Cascade and CNN deep learning ML models.
elements.sent-2411-060: OpenCV is stated to be an open source computer vision library considering especially complicated image processing and detecting objects via algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-061: It is applied in a number of real-world situations.
elements.sent-2411-062: It facilitates improved object recognition, gesture comprehension, and human-computer interaction.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is utilized, for instance, in facial recognition, camera tracking, and motion comprehension \cite{26 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: It also aids with activities like measuring distances and deriving 3D information from multi-camera photos.
elements.sent-2411-065: To put it another way, OpenCV can be thought of as a tool that aids computers in seeing and comprehending the world around them.
elements.sent-2411-066: Examples of this kind of assistance include facial recognition in photos and guiding robots around their environment\cite{16 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: A Python package called CVZone facilitates mediapipe operation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Because of its ability to process both audio and visual input, it simplifies tasks like recognizing faces, tracking hand motions, calculating body positions, and identifying facial features.
elements.sent-2411-069: To put it another way, CVZone facilitates the analysis of sounds and movies, which makes it simpler to perform interesting tasks like hand tracking and facial recognition in videos \cite{17 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, human detection and counting the voters by using this source of libraries possibly a better option as the system would need human features in its tasks.
elements.sent-2411-071: An algorithm to efficiently detect object by subtracting the background and reading its movement to count the voters individually, Mixture of Gaussian 2 (MoG2) is suitable for real time.
elements.sent-2411-072: For every pixel in a video clip, the MoG2 technique tracks multiple Gaussian distributions simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-073: In contrast to MoG, MoG2 preserves unique density functions for every pixel, which enables it to efficiently handle and integrate intricate multi-modal background distributions.
elements.sent-2411-074: According to Mohamed et al. \cite{19 , the Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) approach proves advantageous in outdoor settings as it excels in distinguishing and suppressing non-stationary objects often considered as noise, like moving leaves or shifting sky patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-075: It functions by maintaining a background model that is updated every time a new frame of video is captured \cite{27 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: This feature significantly reduces its memory demands compared to non-recursive methods, enhancing its computational efficiency while maintaining reliability in object detection.
elements.sent-2411-077: The Mixture of Gaussian (MOG) approach was presented by Stauffer and Grimson as a way to describe background information pixel-by-pixel.
elements.sent-2411-078: The method of this approach was to improve the intensity and variance mean for each and every individual pixel in real time, while also changing the background continually.
elements.sent-2411-079: As MoG2 technology determines the appropriate number of Gaussian distributions for each pixel automatically.
elements.sent-2411-080: It might even choose whether or not to detect shadows with it.
elements.sent-2411-081: Because this approach can adapt to different lighting conditions, it works effectively in a variety of scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-082: MOG2 is updated version of MoG, generating similar approach with extra features \cite{18 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: By using this method, the model can adjust to changing lighting and dynamic changes in the surroundings, which helps it distinguish between various background modes that are present in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-084: This image \hyperref[fig:mog1]{Figure~\ref*{fig:mog1 illustrates the background subtraction process.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The process starts with preparing the input video frames, then moves on to background modeling to identify the foreground.
elements.sent-2411-086: The background model is constantly adjusted, and post-processing is used to enhance the accuracy of detecting moving objects by refining the foreground mask.

elements.sent-2411-088: A computer vision-based method for person counting and monitoring entrances and exits inside a scene is presented in the publication "Robust Background Subtraction Based Person's Counting from Overhead View." After testing a number of background reduction techniques with an overhead camera, the study concludes that the Mixture of Gaussian (MoG) algorithm works best for top-down person counting.
elements.sent-2411-089: This method tracks people entering and leaving the area by using a predefined virtual zone.
elements.sent-2411-090: Based on the ground truth of the created dataset with the abridgment of limited subject in constrained environment, the algorithm achieves high accuracy: 98\% for entries and exits within the virtual zone and 98\% for human counting.

elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{phase.png} \caption{All The Phases of the Evaluation Process.} \label{fig:phases}
elements.sent-2411-130: Examining previous studies on computer vision integration, image processing techniques, and automated voter counting systems in election processes is the main goal of the literature review phase.
elements.sent-2411-131: This entails combining various studies, evaluating the advantages and disadvantages, looking at case studies from around the world, and seeing patterns to guide the development of transparent, reliable, and accurate voting systems.
elements.sent-2411-132: Conduct a meticulous and systematic review of a diverse range of academic databases, journals, conference proceedings, and authoritative publications to assemble a comprehensive collection of relevant literature.
elements.sent-2411-133: Organize the gathered literature into distinct themes, encompassing topics such as advanced image processing algorithms, the utilization of computer vision in voting systems, and the evolution of automated voter counting mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-134: Perform a critical synthesis of the identified literature, emphasizing the strengths, weaknesses, trends, and gaps in existing methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-135: This synthesis will provide a nuanced understanding of the current state of automated voter counting systems.
elements.sent-2411-136: Incorporate interdisciplinary research to gain a comprehensive knowledge that encompasses technology, ethics, and government.
elements.sent-2411-137: The second phase endeavors to conduct a meticulously designed experiment emulating a real-world voting scenario.
elements.sent-2411-138: This involves recording the voting process and implementing cutting-edge computer vision techniques for automated voter counting.
elements.sent-2411-139: Establish a controlled voting environment, ensuring its authenticity and representatives of actual voting scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-140: Consider factors such as participant behavior, spatial dynamics, and potential challenges.
elements.sent-2411-141: The device should be placed on a suitable clear view area in front of booth entry.
elements.sent-2411-142: Employ state-of-the-art video recording equipment to capture the entire voting process with meticulous attention to detail, ensuring clarity in individual movements and interactions.
elements.sent-2411-143: It requires high level device for capturing the modern included features.
elements.sent-2411-144: This work employs OpenCV's MOG2 algorithm, which is essential for automated voter counting, to detect people accurately through efficient background subtraction.
elements.sent-2411-145: The incorporation of CVZone improves dynamic motion tracking, and the generation of dynamic lines boosts entry/exit demarcation, lowering miscounts and improving system accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-146: Keeping an eye on how environmental factors affect the precision of the automated counting system when voting in order to conduct a thorough investigation.
elements.sent-2411-147: Such as MOG2 on contradiction to selection of shadow.
elements.sent-2411-148: Systematically extract quantitative data encompassing the number of individuals entering and exiting the voting area throughout the recorded session.
elements.sent-2411-149: The third phase involves a rigorous analysis of the extracted data, comparing automated voter counting results with manually derived ground truth to evaluate the accuracy, efficiency, and reliability of the implemented system.
elements.sent-2411-150: Extract pertinent data points from the recorded video, employing advanced data transformation techniques to ensure compatibility and coherence.
elements.sent-2411-151: Establish a robust ground truth by manually counting individuals in the recorded video, serving as a benchmark for evaluating the accuracy of the automated voter counting system.
elements.sent-2411-152: In the quantitative analysis phase, the evaluation of the automated voter counting system will extend beyond traditional metrics like accuracy and precision.
elements.sent-2411-153: The inclusion of the F1 score adds a layer of sophistication to the assessment by considering both precision and recall simultaneously \cite{22 .}
elements.sent-2411-154: This is crucial, especially in scenarios where imbalanced datasets or equitable consideration of false positives and false negatives are paramount.
elements.sent-2411-155: The F1 score is a metric often used in machine learning and statistics to evaluate the effectiveness of a classification model, especially when dealing with imbalanced datasets or confusion matrices.
elements.sent-2411-156: It offers a balance between these two measurements and is the harmonic mean of recall and precision.
elements.sent-2411-157: Precision (also called positive predictive value) can be calculated by dividing the total number of positive predictions by the number of real positive forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-158: It gauges how accurate positive forecasts are .
elements.sent-2411-159: The mathematical process of positive forecasts \cite{28 are: \begin{equation}\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \label{eq:precision}\end{equation} Recall (also called sensitivity or true positive rate) is the number of true positive predictions divided by the total number of actual positive instances.}
elements.sent-2411-160: It measures the ability of the model to capture all the positive instances.
elements.sent-2411-161: The mathematical process of Recall \cite{29 is: \begin{equation}\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \label{eq:recall}\end{equation} The harmonic mean of recall and precision is then used to compute the F1 score.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The mathematical process of F1 score \cite{30 is: \begin{equation}F1 \text{ Score} = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \label{eq:f1score}\end{equation} The F1 score ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates perfect precision and recall, and 0 indicates the worst performance.}
elements.sent-2411-163: It is a useful metric when there is an uneven class distribution or when both false positives and false negatives are important considerations for the task at hand \cite{22 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: Apply robust statistical methods to assess the significance of disparities between automated and manual counting results, providing a scientific basis for the system's efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-165: Present findings comprehensively, incorporating visualizations, statistical summaries, and insights into the strengths and weaknesses of the automated voter counting system.
elements.sent-2411-166: Propose areas for refinement and potential avenues for future research.
elements.sent-2411-167: The results section presents the findings of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-168: It demonstrate how the automated voter counting system performed under various conditions, with a particular focus on accuracy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-169: The automated voter counting system was developed using a custom dataset, created with the collaboration of friends, simulating a voting environment.
elements.sent-2411-170: The dataset was generated by recording individuals using a camera within this simulated voting setting.
elements.sent-2411-171: The following processes were applied to the recorded data using OpenCV, CVZone, and the MOG2 algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-172: The custom dataset was systematically collected, capturing individuals participating in the voting simulation.
elements.sent-2411-173: Utilizing OpenCV, CVZone, and the MOG2 algorithm, the recorded video frames were processed to identify and count individuals entering and exiting the voting area.

elements.sent-2411-175: Utilizing a custom dataset meticulously gathered during the simulation, the image showcases the successful application of OpenCV, CVZone.
elements.sent-2411-176: The effectiveness of the automated voter counting system was assessed by comparing its output with manually verified ground truth data.
elements.sent-2411-177: As shown in Table 1, the system performed well across multiple test scenarios involving different numbers of participants.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each scenario measured the system’s ability to accurately count both entries and exits within the polling area.

elements.sent-2411-182: Let's define the following: For entering:.
elements.sent-2411-183: True Positives (TP\_enter): 30 (people who both entered and were counted).
elements.sent-2411-184: False Positives (FP\_enter): 0 (people who were counted as entering but did not enter).
elements.sent-2411-185: False Negatives (FN\_enter): 0 (people who entered but were not counted).
elements.sent-2411-186: True Positives (TP\_exit): 29 (people who both exited and were counted).
elements.sent-2411-187: False Positives (FP\_exit): 1 (people who were counted as exiting but did not exit).
elements.sent-2411-188: False Negatives (FN\_exit): 1 (people who exited but were not counted).
elements.sent-2411-189: \begin{equation}Precision = \frac{TP_{exit}}{TP_{exit} + FP_{exit}} = \frac{29}{30}\end{equation} \begin{equation}Recall = \frac{TP_{exit}}{TP_{exit} + FN_{exit}} = \frac{29}{30}\end{equation} \begin{equation}F1_{exit} = 2 \times \frac{Precision \times Recall}{Precision + Recall} = 0.983 = 98.3\%\end{equation} Next step is to calculate precision, recall, and F1 score for both entering and exiting, and then find the average. \begin{equation}Average F1 Score:\end{equation} \begin{equation}Average F1Score = \frac{F1_{enter} + F1_{exit}}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.983}{2} = 0.9915 = 99.15\%\end{equation}.

People Exit 98.3\% High accuracy of 98.3\% in counting people exiting the voting area. elements.sent-2411-192: Moreover, the high accuracy of 98.3\% in counting people exiting reflects the system's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-193: The overall counting average reached an impressive 99.15\%, emphasizing the reliability of the system in monitoring both entry and exit scenarios.
